doc_001: Python is a high-level, general-purpose programming language. Its design philosophy emphasizes code readability with the use of significant indentation. Python supports multiple programming paradigms, including structured, object-oriented, and functional programming.
doc_002: JavaScript is a programming language and core technology of the World Wide Web. It is a high-level, often just-in-time compiled language that conforms to the ECMAScript standard. JavaScript has dynamic typing, prototype-based object-orientation, and first-class functions.
doc_003: Machine learning is a subset of artificial intelligence that focuses on algorithms that can learn from data. Supervised learning uses labeled datasets to train models, while unsupervised learning discovers patterns in unlabeled data.
doc_004: Deep learning uses neural networks with multiple layers to learn hierarchical representations of data. Convolutional neural networks (CNNs) are particularly effective for image recognition tasks.
doc_005: Natural language processing (NLP) enables computers to understand, interpret, and generate human language. Techniques include tokenization, named entity recognition, sentiment analysis, and machine translation.
doc_006: Vector databases store high-dimensional vector embeddings and enable fast similarity search. Common algorithms include HNSW (Hierarchical Navigable Small World) and IVF (Inverted File Index) for approximate nearest neighbor search.
doc_007: SQLite is a C library that implements a self-contained, serverless, zero-configuration SQL database engine. It is the most widely deployed database engine in the world, used in web browsers, mobile phones, and embedded systems.
doc_008: PostgreSQL is an open-source relational database management system. It supports advanced features like JSON storage, full-text search, window functions, and custom extensions.
doc_009: Git is a distributed version control system for tracking changes in source code. Key concepts include commits, branches, merging, rebasing, and remote repositories.
doc_010: Docker containers package applications with their dependencies into standardized units. Container images are built from Dockerfiles and can be shared via container registries like Docker Hub.
doc_011: Kubernetes orchestrates containerized applications across clusters of machines. It handles scaling, load balancing, service discovery, and rolling updates automatically.
doc_012: REST APIs follow representational state transfer architectural constraints. They use HTTP methods (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE) and are stateless, with resources identified by URIs.
doc_013: GraphQL is a query language for APIs that allows clients to request exactly the data they need. Unlike REST, a single endpoint serves all queries, reducing over-fetching and under-fetching.
doc_014: Test-driven development (TDD) is a software development approach where tests are written before code. The cycle is: write a failing test, write minimal code to pass, refactor.
doc_015: Continuous integration (CI) automatically builds and tests code changes. Continuous deployment (CD) extends this by automatically deploying passing builds to production.
doc_016: OAuth 2.0 is an authorization framework that enables applications to obtain limited access to user accounts. It uses access tokens, refresh tokens, and authorization codes.
doc_017: JSON Web Tokens (JWT) are compact tokens for securely transmitting information. A JWT consists of a header, payload, and signature, encoded in Base64URL format.
doc_018: HTTPS uses TLS/SSL to encrypt HTTP traffic. The TLS handshake involves certificate verification, key exchange, and establishing a symmetric encryption session.
doc_019: Microservices architecture decomposes applications into small, independent services. Each service runs in its own process and communicates via lightweight protocols like HTTP or message queues.
doc_020: Event-driven architecture uses events to trigger and communicate between decoupled services. Common patterns include event sourcing, CQRS (Command Query Responsibility Segregation), and pub/sub messaging.
doc_021: React is a JavaScript library for building user interfaces. It uses a virtual DOM for efficient updates and supports component-based architecture with JSX syntax.
doc_022: CSS Grid and Flexbox are layout systems for web design. Grid provides two-dimensional layouts with rows and columns, while Flexbox handles one-dimensional layouts along a single axis.
doc_023: WebAssembly (Wasm) is a binary instruction format for stack-based virtual machines. It enables near-native performance in web browsers for computationally intensive tasks.
doc_024: Redis is an in-memory data structure store used as a database, cache, and message broker. It supports strings, hashes, lists, sets, sorted sets, and streams.
doc_025: Apache Kafka is a distributed event streaming platform. It handles high-throughput, fault-tolerant publish-subscribe messaging and is used for building real-time data pipelines.
doc_026: Transformer models use self-attention mechanisms to process sequential data. BERT uses bidirectional encoding, while GPT uses autoregressive decoding for text generation.
doc_027: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) combines information retrieval with language model generation. Documents are embedded, indexed, and retrieved at query time to ground LLM responses in factual content.
doc_028: Embedding models convert text into dense vector representations. Sentence transformers like all-MiniLM-L6 produce fixed-size embeddings suitable for semantic similarity search.
doc_029: BM25 (Best Matching 25) is a probabilistic retrieval function based on term frequency and inverse document frequency. It is the standard baseline for keyword-based document retrieval.
doc_030: Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF) combines multiple ranked lists by summing reciprocal ranks. It is commonly used to merge dense (vector) and sparse (BM25) retrieval results.
doc_031: Chunk sizing affects retrieval quality. Smaller chunks provide precise matches but may lose context, while larger chunks preserve context but may dilute relevance signals.
doc_032: Hybrid search combines dense vector retrieval with sparse keyword retrieval. This approach captures both semantic similarity and exact term matches for improved recall.
doc_033: Re-ranking uses a cross-encoder model to score query-document pairs more accurately than bi-encoder retrieval. It is computationally expensive and typically applied to a small candidate set.
doc_034: FAISS (Facebook AI Similarity Search) is a library for efficient similarity search of dense vectors. It supports multiple index types including flat, IVF, and HNSW.
doc_035: Prompt engineering involves crafting effective instructions for language models. Techniques include few-shot examples, chain-of-thought reasoning, and system message design.
doc_036: Data pipelines extract, transform, and load data between systems. Modern architectures use tools like Apache Airflow, dbt, or Dagster for orchestration and transformation.
doc_037: Monitoring and observability track system health through metrics, logs, and traces. Tools like Prometheus, Grafana, and OpenTelemetry provide comprehensive observability stacks.
doc_038: Load balancing distributes network traffic across multiple servers. Algorithms include round-robin, least connections, and weighted distribution based on server capacity.
doc_039: Caching strategies like LRU (Least Recently Used), TTL (Time To Live), and write-through improve application performance by reducing repeated expensive operations.
doc_040: Database indexing improves query performance by maintaining sorted data structures. B-tree indexes support range queries, while hash indexes excel at equality lookups.
doc_041: Functional programming emphasizes immutable data, pure functions, and declarative code. Key concepts include higher-order functions, closures, map/reduce, and pattern matching.
doc_042: Concurrency in Python uses threading for I/O-bound tasks and multiprocessing for CPU-bound tasks. The Global Interpreter Lock (GIL) prevents true parallel thread execution in CPython.
doc_043: Async programming in Python uses async/await syntax with the asyncio event loop. It is ideal for I/O-bound concurrent operations like web scraping or API calls.
doc_044: SQL window functions perform calculations across sets of rows related to the current row. Common functions include ROW_NUMBER, RANK, LAG, LEAD, and running aggregates.
doc_045: Full-text search indexes allow efficient text querying. SQLite FTS5 uses a tokenized inverted index with BM25 ranking for fast full-text queries on embedded databases.
doc_046: API rate limiting protects services from abuse by restricting request frequency. Common algorithms include token bucket, sliding window, and fixed window counters.
doc_047: Content-addressable storage uses cryptographic hashes as identifiers. Git objects, IPFS blocks, and Docker layers all use content addressing for deduplication and integrity.
doc_048: Schema migration tools like Alembic, Flyway, and Knex manage database schema changes over time. Migrations are versioned scripts that can be applied or rolled back.
doc_049: Semantic versioning (SemVer) uses MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH numbering. Major changes break compatibility, minor changes add features, and patch changes fix bugs.
doc_050: Code review best practices include keeping changes small, providing constructive feedback, checking for security issues, and verifying test coverage before approval.
doc_051: Python virtual environments isolate project dependencies. Tools include venv (built-in), virtualenv, conda, and poetry for dependency management and environment reproducibility.
doc_052: Type hints in Python use annotations to specify expected types. Tools like mypy, pyright, and pytype perform static type checking to catch errors before runtime.
